grammarRule: Always singular 'gibt' regardless of what follows — takes Akkusativ
grammarRuleDetail: es gibt is a fixed impersonal expression. The verb is always gibt (3rd sg.) even when referring to plural things. The noun following always takes Akkusativ.

usage.en: Is there a supermarket here? (Akk sg.)

grammarRule: Always takes 3rd person singular verb form — same as er/sie/es
grammarRuleDetail: man is an impersonal pronoun used to make general statements without specifying who. It always triggers 3rd person singular verb forms. It cannot be used as an object — use einen (Akk) or einem (Dat).
structureFormula: Man + V(3sg) … | Das macht man nicht.

usage.en: One is not allowed to smoke here.

grammarRule: Always paired with aber or doch — zwar … aber = admittedly … but
grammarRuleDetail: zwar introduces a concession that is then countered by aber or doch. It cannot stand alone without the contrasting part. Position: after verb or at start of clause (triggers V2).
structureFormula: zwar … aber | Das ist zwar teuer, aber gut.

usage.de: Das ist zwar teuer, aber sehr gut.
usage.en: It's admittedly expensive, but very good.

usage.de: Er ist zwar müde, aber er macht weiter.
usage.en: He is tired, it's true, but he carries on.

grammarRule: Multiple uses — in negations means 'at all', in questions 'anyway', in affirmations 'in general'
grammarRuleDetail: überhaupt strengthens negations (nicht überhaupt = not at all) and questions (Warum überhaupt? = Why even?). In positive sentences it generalises.
structureFormula: gar nicht / überhaupt nicht | Warum überhaupt?

usage.en: That doesn't interest me at all.

grammarRule: Fixed greeting phrase used at any time of day in formal contexts.
grammarRuleDetail: 'Guten Tag' is an accusative fixed phrase (short for 'Ich wünsche Ihnen einen guten Tag'). It is neutral and appropriate from mid-morning to early evening.

grammarRule: Informal greeting used with friends, family, or peers.
grammarRuleDetail: 'Hallo' is suitable in informal and semi-formal situations but is generally avoided in very formal professional contexts where 'Guten Tag' is preferred.

grammarRule: Formal farewell used in professional or polite contexts.
grammarRuleDetail: 'Auf Wiedersehen' literally means 'until we see each other again'. It is used in face-to-face situations; 'Auf Wiederhören' is the equivalent on the phone.

usage.en: Goodbye and thank you very much.

grammarRule: Informal farewell used with friends, family, or peers.
grammarRuleDetail: 'Tschüs' is informal and should not be used in formal professional settings. It is widespread across all German-speaking countries.

grammarRule: Formal greeting used in the evening, typically from around 6 pm onwards.
grammarRuleDetail: 'Guten Abend' follows the same accusative fixed-phrase pattern as 'Guten Tag'. Use it when greeting someone in the evening in formal or polite settings.

usage.de: Guten Abend, meine Damen und Herren.

grammarRule: Greeting used in the morning, typically until around midday.
grammarRuleDetail: 'Guten Morgen' follows the same accusative fixed-phrase pattern as 'Guten Tag'. In informal speech it is often shortened to 'Morgen'.

usage.de: Guten Morgen! Wie geht es Ihnen?

grammarRule: Farewell phrase used before going to sleep or very late at night.
grammarRuleDetail: 'Gute Nacht' is an accusative fixed phrase used as a parting greeting at bedtime. It is not used as a general evening greeting — use 'Guten Abend' for that.

grammarRule: Used as a fixed welcome phrase, often followed by 'bei' + dative.
grammarRuleDetail: 'Willkommen' is typically used predicatively as an interjection. When followed by 'bei', the noun takes the dative case: 'Willkommen bei der Firma'.

grammarRule: Fixed expression of gratitude; used on its own or reinforced with 'sehr' or 'schön'.
grammarRuleDetail: 'Danke' is an invariable interjection. It can be strengthened to 'Danke schön' or 'Danke sehr'. The formal equivalent in full is 'Vielen Dank'.

usage.en: Thank you very much, Ms. Fleckenstein.

grammarRule: Formal fixed expression of gratitude; stronger than 'danke'.
grammarRuleDetail: 'Vielen Dank' is an accusative fixed phrase (many thanks). It is used in formal written and spoken communication and is interchangeable with 'Herzlichen Dank' in tone.

usage.en: Thank you very much, Mr. Kostadinov.
Thank you very much for your help.

grammarRule: Farewell phrase used exclusively at the end of phone calls.
grammarRuleDetail: 'Auf Wiederhören' is the telephone equivalent of 'Auf Wiedersehen'. It literally means 'until we hear each other again' and is the standard formal way to end a phone call.

usage.en: Thank you very much and goodbye.

grammarRule: Used to make requests polite or to respond to 'Danke'.
grammarRuleDetail: 'Bitte' can appear before or after the verb in requests to soften them. As a response to thanks it means 'you're welcome'. It is also used to ask someone to repeat: 'Wie bitte?'

grammarRule: Fixed phrase used to ask someone to repeat what they said.
grammarRuleDetail: 'Wie bitte?' is the polite standard way to ask for repetition in German. It is always used as a complete utterance and is more polite than simply saying 'Was?'.

usage.de: Wie bitte? Können Sie das wiederholen?
